coredata.dc:title: Carbon-dots derived from nanodiamond: Photoluminescence tunable nanoparticles for cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiaoyong
Wang, Shiqi
Zhu, Chongyu
Liu, Meiying
Ji, Yan
Feng, Lin
Tao, Lei
Wei, Yen
coredata.dc:description: Water dispersible carbon-dots (CDs) with tunable photoluminescence were synthesized via one-pot hydrothermal oxidation of nanodiamond and subsequently utilized for cell imaging applications. The CDs were characterized by the following techniques including transmission electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, UV–Visible spectroscopy, and fluorescent spectroscopy. Results showed that the size of CDs is mainly distributed at 3–7nm. Many functional groups were introduced on the surface of CDs during hydrothermal oxidation procedure. Cell morphology observation and cell viability measurement demonstrated the good biocompatibility of CDs, suggesting their potential bioimaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanodiamond
Carbon-dots
Tunable photoluminescence
Cell imaging
Biocompatibility

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Observation of cell morphology incubated with CDs
Confocal microscopic imaging of cells using CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Representative TEM image of CDs. Scale bar=10nm. (B) FT-IR spectrum of CDs, stretch bands centered at 3400, 1732, and 1100cm−1 were observed, suggesting the functional groups including COOH, OH and CO were existed on the surface of CDs.
XPS spectra of CDs. (A) Survey scans the spectral region from 0 to 1000eV; (B–D) High-resolution XPS C1s, O1s and N1s narrow scans as a function of electron binding energy (BE).

(A) The normalized PL emission spectra (with progressively increased excitation wavelengths from 345 to 465nm) of CD dispersion in water. Fluorescence microscopy photos of CD suspensions. The photos were obtained by dipping drops of CDs on a glass slide. CD suspensions were excitated by different optical filters (A, I3, and N21). (B–D) fluorescence images were taken using the optical filters A (340–380nm), I3 (450–490nm) and N21 (515–560nm), respectively. Scale bar=50μm.
Microscopy photos of NIH-3T3 cells incubated with 50μgmL−1 of CDs for different time points. (A) 0h, (B) 6h, (C) 12h, (D) 24h, (E) 36h, (F) 48h. The magnification is 100 fold, and scale bar=200μm.

Cellular toxicity and cellular imaging of CDs. (A) Cytotoxicity evaluation of CDs on NIH-3T3 cells, cells were incubated with different concentrations of CDs ranging from 20 to 320μgmL–1 for 24h. No significant cytotoxicity was found, suggesting their potential for bioimaging applications. (B–D) are CLSM images of cells imaged under bright field, 405nm, 458nm excitations, respectively. Cells were incubated with 300μgmL–1 of CDs for 4h. the cell imaging demonstrated that CDs could be uptaken by cells and located in the cytoplasm.
Diagram showing the preparation of CDs via one-pot thermal oxidation ND and the use of CDs for cell imaging. CDs show excitation-dependent PL due to the wide size distribution of CDs.

head.ce:title: Carbon-dots derived from nanodiamond: Photoluminescence tunable nanoparticles for cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences (BNLMS), Key Laboratory of Organic Solids, Laboratory of New Materials, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: ► Carbon-dots was derived from nanodimond. ► Carbon-dots with tunable photoluminescence. ► Carbon-dots show excellent biocompatibility. ► Carbon-dots were used to cell imaging.

Water dispersible carbon-dots (CDs) with tunable photoluminescence were synthesized via one-pot hydrothermal oxidation of nanodiamond and subsequently utilized for cell imaging applications. The CDs were characterized by the following techniques including transmission electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, UV–Visible spectroscopy, and fluorescent spectroscopy. Results showed that the size of CDs is mainly distributed at 3–7nm. Many functional groups were introduced on the surface of CDs during hydrothermal oxidation procedure. Cell morphology observation and cell viability measurement demonstrated the good biocompatibility of CDs, suggesting their potential bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the last decade, fluorescent carbon nanoparticles have attracted great research interest due to their remarkable optical properties, water dispersibility, and biocompatibility [1–3]. Taken advantage of these properties, these nanoparticles have been extensively investigated for bioimaging, biosensing, and catalysts [4–8]. To date, various strategies for the preparation of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles have been developed using organic compounds and carbon materials as carbon precusors [9–15]. In particular, the preparation of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles via one-pot hydrothermal oxidation of carbon materials such as C60, carbon nanotubes, graphite carbon, and graphene oxide has recently been pursed [16–18]. However, to the best of our knowledge, the preparation of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles via hydrothermal oxidation of nanodiamond (ND) has not been reported.

ND is a relative novel class of carbon nanomaterials which can be synthesized on a large scale through detonation method [19,20]. Compared with other carbon nanomaterials, such as carbon black, carbon nanotube, C60, and graphene oxide, ND shows the best biocompatibility to various cells, making it extremely attractive for various biomedical applications [21–35]. Since the first report of the photoluminescence (PL) of ND by Wrachtrup et al., the synthesis and bioimaging applications of fluorescence ND have become one of the most interesting and dynamic research fields [36–40]. Various methods for the preparation of fluorescent ND nanoparticles have thus been developed and extensively investigated for biological imaging in cells and small organisms [41–43]. Among them, the generation of nitrogen-vacancy center in ND by irradiation using a high-energy proton beam and subsequent annealing is the commonly used method for the preparation of fluorescent ND [40]; however, this method suffers from the drawbacks including complex experimental procedure and requirement of expensive equipments. Thus, seeking alternative facile and effective methods for large scale production of fluorescent ND is desirable. Recently, Mochalin and Gogotsi have reported the wet chemistry route for preparation of fluorescent ND by conjugating carboxylated ND nanoparticles with hydrophobic octadecylamine [44]. Compared with the traditional irradiation approach, this wet chemistry method is relative simple and effective, opening up a new door for preparation of fluorescent ND. However, the octadecylamine conjugated ND nanoparticles cannot be dispersed in aqueous media, which severely limited their biomedical applications.

In current work, we reported for the first time, the preparation of water dispersible CDs via one-pot hydrothermal oxidation of ND. The cell imaging applications and biocompatibility of the CDs were also investigated (Scheme 1 ). Compared previous reports, the method described in this work for preparation of ND based fluorescent CDs is rather simple and effectiveness. Thus, obtained CDs showed tunable PL, excellent water dispersibility, and biocompatibility, which are promising for cell imaging applications.

ND was purchased from Beijing Grish Hitech Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). Its diameter is about 30nm according to the supplier. All the other chemical agents were obtained from commercial routes with at least analytical grade. CDs were prepared by hydrothermal oxidation of ND powder using modified hummers method [45]. Briefly, ND (1.0g), NaNO3 (6.0g), and KMnO4 (3.0g) were added to concentrated sulfuric acid (48mL) successively under stirring in ice bath. Then, 100mL of deionized water was poured into the mixture. And then, the mixture was refluxed for 7days. For obtained the fluorescent particles, the dialysis tube against with ethanol, the molecular weight cutoff between 1000 and 7000 was collected and for further characterization.

The fluorescence measurement was made on a PE LS-55 spectrometer equipped with quartz cuvettes of 1cm path length. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained in a transmission mode on a Perkin–Elmer Spectrum 100 spectrometer (Waltham, MA, USA). Typically, 4 scans at a resolution of 1cm−1 were accumulated to obtain one spectrum. UV–Visible absorption spectra were recorded on a UV/Vis/NIR Perkin–Elmer lambda750 spectrometer (Waltham, MA, USA) using quartz curettes of 1cm path length. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a TECNAIG2 20 microscope operated at 200kV. The TEM specimens were made by placing a drop of the nanoparticle suspension on a carbon-coated copper grid. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were taken out using a Nanoscope multiMode SPM (Digital Instruments) with a AS-12 scanner operated in tapping mode IN conjunction with a V-shaped tapping tip (Applied Nanostructures SPM model: ACTA). The images were recorded at a scan rate of 2Hz. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured using a PHI Model Quantera SXM scanning X-ray microprobe, using Al Kα as the excitation source (1486.6eV), and binding energy calibration was based on C1s at 284.8eV. The zeta-potential of CDs in phosphate buffer saline (PBS), fetal bovine serum (FBS) free, and FBS containing cell culture medium were determined using a zeta Plus particle size analyzer (ZetaPlus, Brookhaven Instruments, Holtsville, NY).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Observation of cell morphology incubated with CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Mouse embryo fibroblast (NIH-3T3) cells and adenocarcinomic human alveolar basal epithelial (A549) cells were cultured in Dubecco’s modified eagle medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated FBS, 2mM glutamine, 100UmL−1 penicillin, and 100μgmL−1 of streptomycin. Cell culture was maintained at 37°C in a humidified condition of 95% air and 5% CO2 in culture medium. Culture medium was changed every 3days for maintaining the exponential growth of the cells.

The effect of CDs on NIH-3T3 and A549 cells was first examined by optical microscopy. Briefly, cells were seeded in 6-well microplates at a density of 1×105 cellsmL−1 in 2mL of respective media containing 10% FBS. After cell attachment, plates were washed with PBS, and the cells were treated with complete cell culture medium, or cell culture media containing 50μgmL−1 of CDs for 6, 12, 24, 36, 48h. Then, all samples were washed three times with PBS to remove the uninternalized nanoparticles. Cells were observed by using an optical microscopy (Leica, DMI3000B, Germany), and photos were taken using a charge-coupled device (CCD). The overall magnification was ×100.

Cell viability of CDs on NIH-3T3 and A549 cells was determined by counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay according to our previous report [29,46]. Briefly, cells were seeded in 96-well microplates at a density of 5×104 cellsmL−1 in 160μL of respective media containing 10% FBS. After 24h of cell attachment, the cells were incubated with 20, 40, 80, 160, 320μgmL−1 of CDs nanoparticles for 24h. Then nanoparticles were removed, and cells were washed with PBS for three times. 10μL of CCK-8 dye and 100μL of DMEM cell culture media were added to each well and incubated for 2h at 37°C. Plates were then analyzed with a microplate reader (VictorШ, Perkin–Elmer). Measurements of dye absorbance were carried out at 450nm, with the reference wavelength at 620nm. The values were proportional to the number of live cells. The percent reduction in CCK-8 dye was compared to controls (cells not exposed to nanoparticles), which represented 100% CCK-8 dye reduction. Three replicate wells were used for each control and test concentrations per microplate, and the experiment was repeated three times. Cell survival was expressed as absorbance relative to that of untreated controls. Results are presented as mean±standard deviation (SD).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal microscopic imaging of cells using CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Confocal microscope images were taken to evaluate the cell imaging applications of CDs. Briefly, on the day prior to treatment, cells were seeded in a glass bottom dishwith a density of 1×105 cells per dish. On the day of treatment, the cells were incubated with CDs at a final concentration of 300μgmL−1 for 4h at 37°C. Afterward, the cells were washed three times with PBS to remove the CDs and then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10min at room temperature. Cell images were taken with a Laser Scanning Confocal Microscope (LCSM) Zesis 710 3-channels (Zesis, Germany) with the excitation wavelengths of 405nm and 458nm.

The representative TEM image of CDs is shown in Fig. 1 A. It can be seen that the size of most CDs is smaller than 10nm, close to the diameters of individual ND nanoparticles as described in our previous reports [47]. The size and size distribution of CDs were also characterized by AFM. AFM image shows that the height of CDs is mainly distributed at 3–7nm (SFig. 1), which is well consistent with the TEM results.

The functional groups existed on CDs were characterized by FT-IR spectra. As shown in Fig. 1B, stretch bands centered at 3400, 1732, and 1100cm−1 were observed, evidencing the functional groups including OH, COOH and CO were introduced on the surface of CDs during the oxidation procedure, thus leading to excellent water dispersibility of CDs. The surface chemistry properties of CDs were further demonstrated by XPS spectra (Fig. 2 ). A survey with XPS scan ranging from 0 to 1000eV showed four peaks at 284.8, 289.1, 399.2, and 531.2eV are attributed to elements of C, N, and O, respectively. In the spectrum of C1s, two peaks located at 284.8 and 289.1eV are corresponding to the CC and hydroxyl carbon, respectively (Fig. 2B). Among them, the peak at 284.8eV originates from sp3-hybridized carbon atoms (CC). The higher binding energy signals (289.1eV) can be ascribed to the C1s of hydroxyl carbon COH, CO and COOH (Fig. 2B). The O1s region at 531.2eV can be ascribed to oxygen atoms on the carbon surface (Fig. 2C). N1s spectrum in Fig. 2D further evidenced the existence of oxygen groups on the surface of CDs. Furthermore, calculating the content of elements on surface of CDs by area of each element, we can find that the weight contents of C, O, N, and S are 56.53%, 41.14%, 0.8%, and 1.54%, respectively. It should be noted that the relative intensity of the C1s peak of CDs was significantly decreased, while O1s peak increased as compared to the survey spectra obtained from NDOH and NDCOOH, indicating much oxygen-containing functional groups were introduced on the surface of CDs [48]. The stability of nanoparticles in physiological solution is of great importance for their biomedical applications. Here, we demonstrated that zeta-potential values of CDs in PBS, FBS free, and FBS containing cell culture medium are −32.7, −34.2, and −36.4eV, respectively. The negative values of zeta-potential are likely ascribed to the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs, which resulting in their excellent water dispersibility in physiological solution.

In the UV–Visible absorption spectrum (SFig. 2), a typical absorption peak at 314nm, similar to other carbon-dots, was also observed [5]. The PL spectrum of SFig. 2 also shows a strong emission peak at 456nm when excitated at 365nm. The full width at half maximum is about 100nm, which approximates to that of most reported carbon nanodots [5]. Insets of SFig. 2 show photographs of CD aqueous suspensions taken under visible (left) and UV lights (right), strong blue fluorescent color could be clearly observed by naked eye under excitation of 365nm.

SFig. 3 and Fig. 3 A show the PL emission spectra and normalized PL emission spectra of CDs using progressively increased excitation wavelengths from 345 to 465nm. As the excitation wavelengths increased, the PL emission peaks shift from 439 to 520nm (Fig. 3A, SFig. 3). The excitation-dependent PL behavior has also been reported in previous studies of other fluorescent carbon-based nanoparticles, which are likely due to the different size and surface properties of CDs [5,6,13,15,49]. Though the mechanism of generation of PL is unknown, the small size and surface defect of CDs may contribute to the PL of CDs [5,50], which is significant different from the previous reports for the generation of nitrogen-vacancy center in ND by irradiation [1].

The excitation-dependent PL properties of CDs were further evidenced by fluorescence microscopy. Different fluorescence can be observed by using different optical filters. As shown in Fig. 3B–D, blue, green, and red fluorescent images were obtained, while the CD suspensions were excitated by optical filters (A, I3, and N21). The excitation wavelengths of these optical filters are 340–380nm, 450–490nm, and 515–560nm, respectively. Like many other fluorescent carbon-dots, CDs show outstanding photostability [43]. No significant photobleaching was observed during continuous excitated at 365nm for 30min (SFig. 4), which was important for their bioimaging applications.

To evaluate cell imaging applications of CDs, their biocompatibility was subsequently evaluated [51]. Fig. 4 shows the morphology of NIH-3T3 cells when they incubated with 50μgmL–1 of CDs for different time (0–48h). It can be seen that cells kept their normal morphology after incubated with CDs for different time. Cells still kept normal morphology even they were incubated with CDs for 48h, (Fig. 3F). Moreover, as shown in Fig. 4, cells still kept their proliferation capability when incubated with CDs. These results given us the preliminary impression was that CDs are biocompatible with cells. Cell viability results from CCK-8 assay further demonstrated the excellent biocompatibility of CDs. As shown in Fig. 5 A, no obvious cell viability decrease was observed via cell viability examination. Even the concentration of CDs reached up to 320μgmL−1, the cell viability was still greater than 90%. Furthermore, the excellent good biocompatibility of CDs was also evidenced using A549 cells. As shown in SFig. 5D, cell viability of CDs to A549 cells was still greater than 90% even the incubation time was extended to 48h.

In vitro cell imaging of CDs using CLSM shown in Fig. 5B–D was further exploited to demonstrate their biomedical applications. The cell internalized CDs can be excited by laser with 405 and 458nm excitation, and emitted green and green-yellow fluorescent. The fluorescent areas were overlayed on the locations of cells (Fig. 5B), indicating that CDs could uptake by cells and accumulated in cells. From CLSM images, many dark areas were also found within the cells, which may be the locations of cell nucleus. Based on CLSM images, conclusion could be drawn that CDs could translocate into cells and locate at cytoplasm. As compared with the conventional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, the CDs with photoluminescence, water dispersibility and excellent biocompatibility, should be alternative and promising agents for bioimaging applications [52].

In conclusion, water dispersible and biocompatible CDs have been successfully prepared via a straightforward one-pot hydrothermal method. The strong and tunable PL of CDs can be utilized for cell imaging. Given the excellent water dispersibility and biocompatibility of CDs, such types of materials should be promising for bioimaging applications. The separation of the CDs to obtain more colorful fluorescent CDs using dialysis tube with different molecular weight cutoff and the utilization of these CDs for further biomedical applications are carrying out in our lab.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21134004, 21104039 and 21201108), and the National 973 Project (No. 2011CB935700), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2011M500280).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2013.01.063.
